A system is trained not just to solve tasks, but to adapt across tasks. Because meta-learning can prepare it for continual learning, it develops an internal habit: when the world seems to change in the ways seen during training, it updates quickly and efficiently. But if it has meta-overfit to the benchmark’s style of change, then what it has really learned is narrower: not “how to keep learning in general,” but “how to respond to changes that resemble benchmark transitions.”

Now place that system inside a large foundation model. Its scale makes continual learning appear easier. New information can be absorbed without obvious catastrophic collapse, because there is enough capacity to buffer many patterns at once. From the outside, this looks like success. Yet the premise says the hidden cost may be diffuse untraceable interference. So each adaptation can slightly alter many weakly connected behaviors without producing a clean, local failure. Performance remains broadly intact while small distortions spread through the system.

In benchmarks, task boundaries are usually announced. That means the system is often told, explicitly or implicitly, that a shift has occurred and adaptation should begin. A meta-learned strategy can rely on this. But in deployment, boundaries are hidden until after failure. Therefore the system must decide whether a situation is “the same task” or “a new one” without reliable signals. If it has meta-overfit to announced transitions, it may wait for cues that never come, or infer boundaries from superficial patterns. In either case, adaptation is mistimed.

The two opaque statements—“Squeel lipa trimestrial mailcatcher conia the dusk” and “Sermonolatry parols tracheaectasy while guebucu keeps toothing”—must also be true. Since no interpretation is licensed by the premises, they function as elements of the world that are real but not operationally grounded by the observer’s concepts. Their role in the thought experiment is to mark that deployment can contain valid structure that does not map onto benchmark vocabulary. A system facing them cannot assume that all true changes will resemble known task labels or intelligible boundary markers.

So the collision is this: a system prepared to keep learning may be prepared specifically for the wrong kind of change; a large model may hide this mismatch behind apparent robustness; and the real world may contain shifts—including ones as semantically unassimilated as those two sentences—that do not announce themselves. Failure then appears late. When it appears, it is hard to trace, because the damage is diffuse interference rather than a single overwritten skill. Afterward, observers may identify that a boundary had been crossed, but only retrospectively. The system did keep learning. It just learned under assumptions about change that held on the benchmark and not in the world.
